content.problemStatement:
Chef recently organised a **Grand Feast** where he served nnn **different dishes** to his friends.

Each friend picked their favourite dish, and now Chef has a list of all the dishes chosen.

Chef is curious:


Which dishes were so popular that they were chosen by more than ⌊n/3⌋⌊n/3⌋⌊n/3⌋ friends? Print the dishes in increasing order of popularity of dish among the friends.
Can you help Chef find these **superstar dishes**?

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindSuperstarDishesfindSuperstarDishesfindSuperstarDishes – This function finds all dishes that appear more than ⌊n/3⌋ times.

Parameters
aaa : An array representing the dishes chosen by friends.
nnn : The number of dishes.
Return Value
This function **returns the array in ascending order**.
Constraints
1≤n≤5×1041 \leq n \leq 5 \times 10^41≤n≤5×104
−109≤a[i]≤109−10^9 \leq a[i] \leq 10^9−109≤a[i]≤109
The output may contain **at most two dishes** (by pigeonhole principle)

Input Format

The first line of each test case contains a single integer nnn — the number of dishes.
The next line contains nnn space-separated integers a[i]a[i]a[i] — the dishes chosen by friends.


Output Format

For each test case, print on a new line all dishes that were chosen by **more than ⌊n/3⌋ friends**, in **increasing order**.
If no such dish exists, print nothing for that test case.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer n, the number of dishes, and the second line contains n space-separated integers a[i], the dishes chosen by friends.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print on a new line all dishes that were chosen by more than ⌊n/3⌋ friends, in increasing order, separated by a single space. If no such dish exists, output an empty line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: n = 6, floor(6/3)=2. Both 1 and 2 appear 3 times (>2). They are printed in increasing order.
n = 5, floor(5/3)=1. No element appears more than once, so the line is empty.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using the Majority Vote algorithm, a variation of the Boyer-Moore Majority Vote algorithm. We first find two candidates for the most frequent dishes. Then, we validate these candidates by counting their occurrences in the array. Finally, we print the dishes that occur more than ⌊n/3⌋ times in increasing order.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, O(T*n) total. The algorithm iterates over the array twice: once to find the candidates and once to validate them.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space used does not grow with the size of the input, as we only use a constant amount of space to store the candidates and their counts.
